title: Stanford Opens $1M in Seed Grants for AI in Teaching and Learning
subtitle: AIMES and the Stanford Accelerator for Learning will fund course redesigns, classroom research, and thought leadership on how generative AI should reshape higher education.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Stanford announced a $1 million seed-grant program on April 21 to fund faculty, students, and staff projects that rethink how artificial intelligence fits into college teaching. The program is run jointly by AI Meets Education at Stanford (AIMES) and the Stanford Accelerator for Learning, and is split across three funding streams: Course and Curriculum Grants of up to $100,000 for redesigning whole courses around AI, Innovation with Evidence Grants up to $50,000 for classroom studies of specific AI tools, and Thought Leadership Grants for scholarly work on critical issues in AI and education. Proposals are due May 15, with info sessions on April 24 and May 7.

The program's structure is more interesting than the dollar figure. By separately funding course design, empirical evaluation, and intellectual framing, Stanford is treating AI-in-education as a research problem with distinct experimental, implementation, and policy arms — rather than as a technology-adoption rollout. The Innovation with Evidence track in particular commits grantees to generating actual classroom data, which the field has been short on: most public claims about AI tutoring performance come from vendor-run studies or small pilots, not from peer-reviewed university research programs with control groups.

The timing matters because the policy environment is shifting fast. The US Department of Education finalized a rule on April 13 prioritizing AI literacy and ethical-use research in competitive education grants, and 49 US states have introduced AI-in-schools legislation this year. Universities that generate credible evidence about what AI does and does not do in classrooms will have outsized influence over what counts as 'appropriate use' in those policy documents. Stanford is positioning itself — and a handful of peer institutions doing similar programs — to supply that evidence.

For students and early-career educators, the takeaway is that 'how should AI be used in classrooms' is now a live research area with real funding behind it. If you teach, tutor, or TA, this is the right moment to document what you try and what works — the field has more questions than it has published answers, and careful practitioner notes are currently worth more than another think piece. Stanford's program is one of several; if you are affiliated with a research university, it is worth asking whether your own institution has an equivalent pot of money.
